title: Participle clauses: -ing, perfect and -ed
lesson: Participle clauses let us combine two ideas economically by reducing one of them to a participle, with no separate subject of its own. A present (-ing) participle expresses an action happening at the same time as the main verb ('Walking home, she met an old friend') or, more loosely, a result or reason. A perfect participle ('Having finished the report, he went home') makes clear that one action was completed before the next began. A past (-ed) or passive participle reduces a passive clause ('Built in 1890, the bridge still stands'). The same mechanism reduces a relative clause: 'The man who is sitting over there' becomes 'The man sitting over there', and 'a letter that was written in haste' becomes 'a letter written in haste'. Crucially, the understood subject of the participle must be the subject of the main clause, or a dangling modifier results.

items.prompt: She was walking home. She met an old friend.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a present (-ing) participle clause for the simultaneous action, beginning with 'Walking'.
answer: Walking home, she met an old friend.
Walking home she met an old friend
items.explanation: Two actions by the same subject happening at the same time are joined by an -ing clause: 'Walking home' replaces 'While she was walking home'. The subject 'she' is shared, so no second subject is needed.

items.prompt: He finished the report. Then he went home.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause to show the first action was completed first, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having finished the report, he went home.
Having finished the report he went home
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having finished' signals that the report was completed before he left, making the sequence explicit in a way a plain -ing clause ('Finishing the report') could not.

items.prompt: The bridge was built in 1890. It still carries traffic today.
items.instruction: Combine using an -ed (passive) participle clause, beginning with 'Built'.
answer: Built in 1890, the bridge still carries traffic today.
Built in 1890 the bridge still carries traffic today
items.explanation: A passive clause ('which was built in 1890') reduces to the past participle alone: 'Built in 1890'. The understood subject is 'the bridge', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The man who is sitting over there is my old history teacher.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ing participle) instead of 'who is sitting'.
answer: The man sitting over there is my old history teacher.
The man sitting over there is my old history teacher
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'who/which + be + -ing' can be reduced to the bare participle: 'who is sitting' becomes 'sitting'. This is a reduced relative clause, not an adverbial one.

items.prompt: Because she did not know the password, she could not log in.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a negative present participle clause, beginning with 'Not'.
answer: Not knowing the password, she could not log in.
Not knowing the password she could not log in
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because she did not know') reduces to a participle clause expressing reason; the negative is formed by placing 'not' before the participle: 'Not knowing the password'.

items.prompt: The fire spread to the upper floors. It forced hundreds of residents onto the street.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a present (-ing) participle clause to express the result/consequence, ending with a clause beginning '…, forcing…'.
answer: The fire spread to the upper floors, forcing hundreds of residents onto the street.
The fire spread to the upper floors forcing hundreds of residents onto the street
items.explanation: An -ing clause placed after the main clause can express a result or consequence of it: 'forcing hundreds of residents onto the street' = 'and this forced…'. Unlike a simultaneous -ing clause, the participle here describes the outcome that the main event brings about, with 'the fire' (the main subject) understood as its subject.

items.prompt: He had been warned about the risks. He nevertheless chose to invest everything.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect passive participle clause for the completed prior event, beginning with 'Having been'.
answer: Having been warned about the risks, he nevertheless chose to invest everything.
Having been warned about the risks he nevertheless chose to invest everything
items.explanation: A passive clause that is also prior in time reduces to the perfect passive participle 'Having been + past participle': 'Having been warned' = 'Although he had been warned'. The 'Having been' form simultaneously marks the action as both passive (he was the recipient of the warning) and completed before the main verb.

items.prompt: The report, which had been commissioned at great expense, was quietly shelved.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause built on a perfect/passive participle (drop 'which had been').
answer: The report, commissioned at great expense, was quietly shelved.
The report commissioned at great expense was quietly shelved
items.explanation: A non-defining relative clause with 'which had been + past participle' reduces to the bare past participle: 'which had been commissioned' becomes 'commissioned'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the report', not an adverbial one, so the commas of the original relative clause are retained.

items.prompt: When he was questioned by reporters, the minister denied any wrongdoing.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'When' but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle.
answer: When questioned by reporters, the minister denied any wrongdoing.
When questioned by reporters the minister denied any wrongdoing
items.explanation: A subordinating conjunction (when, while, once, if, until) can be kept in front of a participle clause for precision: 'When he was questioned' reduces to 'When questioned', dropping the subject and the form of 'be'. The understood subject remains 'the minister', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: The negotiations finally collapsed. The two sides remained far apart on every key issue.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'with + noun + participle' absolute construction to give the attendant circumstances, placed after the main clause (', with the two sides…').
answer: The negotiations finally collapsed, with the two sides remaining far apart on every key issue.
The negotiations finally collapsed with the two sides remaining far apart on every key issue
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'With + noun + -ing participle' supplies attendant circumstances and, crucially, has its own subject ('the two sides'), so it does not need to share the subject of the main clause. This is why it does not dangle even though its subject differs from 'the negotiations'.

items.prompt: Because she had spent years abroad, she found her home town strangely unfamiliar.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a perfect (active) participle clause expressing cause, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having spent years abroad, she found her home town strangely unfamiliar.
Having spent years abroad she found her home town strangely unfamiliar
items.explanation: When the reason for the main event lies in a completed earlier action, the perfect participle 'Having + past participle' expresses both the cause and its priority in time: 'Having spent years abroad' = 'Because she had spent years abroad'. A plain -ing clause ('Spending years abroad') would wrongly suggest the two events were simultaneous rather than one preceding the other.

items.prompt: The novel was translated into thirty languages. It made its author a household name.
items.instruction: Combine using an -ed (passive) reduced relative clause modifying 'novel', placed between commas (', translated into thirty languages,').
answer: The novel, translated into thirty languages, made its author a household name.
The novel translated into thirty languages made its author a household name
items.explanation: A non-defining relative clause ('which was translated into thirty languages') reduces to the bare past participle 'translated into thirty languages', here embedded mid-sentence between commas as a reduced relative modifying 'the novel'. This is adnominal (it modifies the noun), not adverbial, which is why the commas of the relative clause are kept and the participle sits next to its noun.

items.prompt: Once it has been switched on, the machine calibrates itself automatically.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'Once' but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle.
answer: Once switched on, the machine calibrates itself automatically.
Once switched on the machine calibrates itself automatically
items.explanation: The conjunction 'Once' can be retained in front of a reduced participle clause for temporal precision: 'Once it has been switched on' reduces to 'Once switched on', dropping the subject and the forms of 'be'. The understood subject is 'the machine', the subject of the main clause, so the clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: The storm raged for hours. Rain lashed the windows the whole time.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'with + noun + participle' absolute construction for the attendant circumstances, placed after the main clause (', with rain lashing…').
answer: The storm raged for hours, with rain lashing the windows.
The storm raged for hours with rain lashing the windows
items.explanation: The 'With + noun + -ing participle' absolute construction adds simultaneous attendant detail and carries its own subject ('rain'), which need not match the main subject ('the storm'). Because the participle has an explicit subject of its own, it does not dangle despite describing a different agent from the main clause.

items.prompt: She had spent a decade working in Tokyo. She found the move back to London surprisingly difficult.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect active participle clause to give the completed background reason, beginning with 'Having spent'.
answer: Having spent a decade working in Tokyo, she found the move back to London surprisingly difficult.
Having spent a decade working in Tokyo she found the move back to London surprisingly difficult
items.explanation: The perfect active participle 'Having spent' reduces a completed prior clause ('Because she had spent a decade…') and here conveys both anteriority and reason: the years in Tokyo were over before, and account for, the difficulty of the move. Unlike a plain -ing clause ('Spending a decade…'), the perfect form makes the action explicitly finished before the main verb. The understood subject is 'she', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The novel was translated into more than forty languages. It made its author a global celebrity.
items.instruction: Combine using a non-defining reduced relative clause built on an -ed participle, placed mid-sentence after the subject (', translated into…,').
answer: The novel, translated into more than forty languages, made its author a global celebrity.
The novel translated into more than forty languages made its author a global celebrity
items.explanation: A non-defining relative clause ('which was translated into more than forty languages') reduces to the bare past participle 'translated', set off by paired commas because it adds non-essential information about 'the novel'. Embedded between the subject and its verb, the participle phrase modifies the noun it follows; the commas signal that it is a reduced relative clause, not an adverbial one.

items.prompt: As soon as it is switched on, the device begins recording automatically.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping a subordinating conjunction but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle, beginning with 'Once switched on'.
answer: Once switched on, the device begins recording automatically.
Once switched on the device begins recording automatically
items.explanation: A subordinator such as 'once', 'when', 'until' or 'if' can be retained in front of a reduced passive clause for precision: 'As soon as it is switched on' becomes 'Once switched on', dropping the subject 'it' and the form of 'be'. The retained conjunction keeps the temporal meaning explicit, while the understood subject stays 'the device', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: Because it is situated on a steep hillside, the village is almost inaccessible by car.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present participle clause of an underlying passive, beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being situated on a steep hillside, the village is almost inaccessible by car.
Being situated on a steep hillside the village is almost inaccessible by car
items.explanation: A causal clause whose verb is stative-passive ('Because it is situated…') reduces to 'Being + past participle', which expresses reason: 'Being situated on a steep hillside' = 'Because it is situated…'. 'Being' carries the present, ongoing state, and 'situated' supplies the passive meaning. The understood subject is 'the village', the subject of the main clause, so there is no dangling modifier.

items.prompt: The hikers pressed on towards the summit. Their boots were caked with mud.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'with + noun + -ed (past) participle' absolute construction giving the attendant circumstances, placed after the main clause (', with their boots…').
answer: The hikers pressed on towards the summit, with their boots caked with mud.
The hikers pressed on towards the summit with their boots caked with mud
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'With + noun + past participle' supplies attendant circumstances using a passive (-ed) participle: 'with their boots caked with mud' = 'and their boots were caked with mud'. Like its -ing counterpart, it carries its own subject ('their boots'), so it need not share the subject of the main clause ('the hikers') and therefore does not dangle, even though the two subjects differ.

items.prompt: Because she had lived in Tokyo for a decade, she spoke the language like a native.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a perfect participle clause to show the prior, completed situation, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lived in Tokyo for a decade, she spoke the language like a native.
Having lived in Tokyo for a decade she spoke the language like a native
items.explanation: A causal clause referring to a completed prior stretch of time reduces to the perfect participle 'Having + past participle': 'Because she had lived' becomes 'Having lived'. The perfect form (rather than plain 'Living') makes clear the decade of residence preceded and explains the present skill. The understood subject is 'she', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: The weather was fine, so the whole family decided to eat lunch in the garden.
items.instruction: Combine using an absolute construction (a participle clause that keeps its own subject) for the reason, beginning with 'The weather'.
answer: The weather being fine, the whole family decided to eat lunch in the garden.
The weather being fine the whole family decided to eat lunch in the garden
items.explanation: An absolute construction keeps its own subject ('the weather') and attaches a participle ('being') to it, modifying the whole main clause as a reason. Because it has a subject of its own, it does not need to share the subject of the main clause ('the whole family') and so does not dangle. This is the present-participle absolute, distinct from a 'with + noun + participle' phrase.

items.prompt: I picked up the parcel that was waiting for me at the front desk.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ing participle) modifying the object 'the parcel', dropping 'that was'.
answer: I picked up the parcel waiting for me at the front desk.
I picked up the parcel waiting for me at the front desk
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'that/which + be + -ing' reduces to the bare present participle even when the noun it modifies is the object of the main verb: 'the parcel that was waiting' becomes 'the parcel waiting'. The participle attaches directly to 'parcel', not to the subject 'I', so no dangling occurs. This is a reduced relative clause, not an adverbial one.

items.prompt: As soon as it is completed, the new terminal will double the airport's capacity.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping a conjunction but reducing the time clause to a past (passive) participle, beginning with 'Once'.
answer: Once completed, the new terminal will double the airport's capacity.
Once completed the new terminal will double the airport's capacity
items.explanation: A subordinating conjunction of time can be kept before a participle clause for precision: 'As soon as it is completed' reduces to 'Once completed', dropping the subject and the form of 'be'. The understood subject of the participle is 'the new terminal', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle. 'Once' here means 'as soon as / when'.

items.prompt: If it is handled with care, this antique clock will outlast its owner.
items.instruction: Rewrite the conditional clause as a past (passive) participle clause of condition, beginning with 'Handled'.
answer: Handled with care, this antique clock will outlast its owner.
Handled with care this antique clock will outlast its owner
items.explanation: A conditional 'if'-clause in the passive can reduce to a bare past participle expressing condition: 'If it is handled with care' becomes 'Handled with care' = 'If/Provided it is handled with care'. The understood subject is 'this antique clock', the main-clause subject, so the participle does not dangle. Context (the modal 'will') signals that the meaning is conditional rather than purely temporal.

items.prompt: Because she was short of time, she decided to skip the introduction entirely.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a fronted present (-ing) participle clause expressing cause, beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being short of time, she decided to skip the introduction entirely.
Being short of time she decided to skip the introduction entirely
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because she was short of time') reduces to an -ing participle clause expressing reason, with the participle 'being' standing in for 'was': 'Being short of time' = 'As/Since she was short of time'. This fronted -ing clause states the cause of the main action, and its understood subject is 'she', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: She arrived at the station. She immediately realised she had left her ticket at home.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle of the verb of motion to mark the completed first action, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having arrived at the station, she immediately realised she had left her ticket at home.
Having arrived at the station she immediately realised she had left her ticket at home
items.explanation: With verbs of motion such as 'arrive', the perfect participle 'Having arrived' shows that the movement was fully completed before the next event, making the sequence unambiguous. A plain -ing participle ('Arriving at the station') would suggest the two events happened at the same moment, which is not the intended meaning here.

items.prompt: The stadium that is currently being renovated will reopen to the public next spring.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause based on 'being + past participle' (drop 'that is currently').
answer: The stadium being renovated will reopen to the public next spring.
The stadium being renovated will reopen to the public next spring
items.explanation: A relative clause in the continuous passive ('that is being renovated') reduces to 'being + past participle': 'being renovated'. Unlike a bare past participle ('the stadium renovated', which reads as already finished), the 'being' form preserves the sense that the action is still in progress.

items.prompt: The harvest was safely gathered in. The villagers began their week-long celebration.
items.instruction: Combine using an absolute construction of the form 'noun + past participle' at the front, beginning with 'The harvest'.
answer: The harvest safely gathered in, the villagers began their week-long celebration.
The harvest safely gathered in the villagers began their week-long celebration
items.explanation: An absolute construction of the form 'noun + past participle' ('The harvest safely gathered in') has its own subject ('the harvest') and so does not share the subject of the main clause. It supplies the background circumstance — here, the completed condition — against which the main event takes place, and is largely confined to formal or literary writing.

items.prompt: Reading the email, he sensed at once that something was badly wrong.
items.instruction: Make the participle clause more precise by inserting the conjunction 'On' before an -ing form to mean 'at the moment of', beginning with 'On reading'.
answer: On reading the email, he sensed at once that something was badly wrong.
On reading the email he sensed at once that something was badly wrong
items.explanation: The pattern 'On + -ing' means 'at the moment of doing / as soon as one does': 'On reading the email' = 'As soon as he read the email'. Keeping the preposition 'On' in front of the participle pins the action to the precise instant, which a bare -ing clause leaves vaguer; the understood subject remains 'he', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: His task was finally completed. He allowed himself to relax for the first time in weeks.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction with its own noun subject + past participle (no 'with'), beginning with 'His task'.
answer: His task completed, he allowed himself to relax for the first time in weeks.
His task completed he allowed himself to relax for the first time in weeks
items.explanation: The nominative absolute 'His task completed' carries its own subject ('His task') followed by a past participle, equivalent to 'Now that his task was completed'. Because the absolute supplies its own subject, it need not share the subject of the main clause ('he'), and unlike the 'with'-absolute it takes no introductory preposition.

items.prompt: She had never studied the local dialect. She struggled to follow the villagers' conversations.
items.instruction: Combine using a negative perfect participle clause to express the cause/reason for the main clause, beginning with 'Never'.
answer: Never having studied the local dialect, she struggled to follow the villagers' conversations.
Never having studied the local dialect she struggled to follow the villagers' conversations
items.explanation: A negative perfect participle places the negator before the perfect participle: 'Never having studied' (= 'Because she had never studied'). It marks the cause as prior to and completed before the main clause, with the understood subject 'she' matching the main subject, so it does not dangle.

items.prompt: Walking down the street, the trees were a beautiful sight. (The intended subject is 'I', not 'the trees'.)
items.instruction: Correct the dangling modifier by rewriting so that the understood subject of 'Walking' is also the subject of the main clause, beginning with 'Walking down the street, I'.
answer: Walking down the street, I thought the trees were a beautiful sight.
Walking down the street I thought the trees were a beautiful sight
items.explanation: The original dangles because 'Walking' has no logical subject in the main clause: as written, the trees are doing the walking. A participle clause must share its subject with the main clause, so the fix is to make 'I' the subject of the main verb ('I thought…'). Adding a separate subject to the main clause, rather than the participle, is what removes the dangle.

items.prompt: Because we know how strongly you feel about this, we have decided to postpone the vote.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a participle clause introduced by 'Given', beginning with 'Given'.
answer: Given how strongly you feel about this, we have decided to postpone the vote.
Given how strongly you feel about this we have decided to postpone the vote
items.explanation: The past participle 'Given' has become a fixed conjunction-like marker meaning 'considering' or 'in view of'; it introduces the grounds for the main statement and can be followed by a noun phrase, a 'that'-clause, or a 'wh-' clause ('Given how strongly…'). Unlike an ordinary participle, this idiomatic 'Given' is not felt to dangle, because it no longer requires its subject to match the main clause.

items.prompt: The weather was extremely cold. We decided to abandon the climb.
items.instruction: Combine using an absolute construction (noun + participle, with its own subject and no conjunction), beginning with 'The weather'.
answer: The weather being extremely cold, we decided to abandon the climb.
The weather being extremely cold we decided to abandon the climb
items.explanation: An absolute construction has its own overt subject ('The weather') followed by a participle ('being'), and stands grammatically apart from the main clause, supplying a reason or attendant circumstance. Because it carries its own subject, it does not need to share the subject of the main clause and therefore cannot dangle, unlike a bare '-ing' clause.

items.prompt: After the contract had been signed, there was no way for either party to withdraw.
items.instruction: Rewrite the time clause as a participle clause keeping the conjunction 'Once' and a past participle, beginning with 'Once'.
answer: Once signed, the contract left neither party any way to withdraw.
Once signed the contract left neither party any way to withdraw
items.explanation: The conjunction 'Once' can be kept in front of a reduced passive clause: 'Once it had been signed' becomes 'Once signed', dropping the subject and the form of 'be'. The understood subject of the participle must be the subject of the main clause, so the main clause is recast around 'the contract' ('the contract left…') to avoid a dangling modifier.

items.prompt: Anyone who is found cheating in the examination will be disqualified.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause with a past (passive) participle in place of 'who is found'.
answer: Anyone found cheating in the examination will be disqualified.
Anyone found cheating in the examination will be disqualified
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'who/which + be + past participle' reduces to the bare past participle: 'who is found' becomes 'found'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying the pronoun 'anyone', not an adverbial participle clause, so no comma is used and the word order of the noun phrase is preserved.

items.prompt: Once it has been translated into English, the manual will reach a far wider audience.
items.instruction: Reduce the time clause to a participle clause, keeping the conjunction 'Once' and beginning 'Once translated'.
answer: Once translated into English, the manual will reach a far wider audience.
Once translated into English the manual will reach a far wider audience
items.explanation: As with 'when', 'while' and 'until', the conjunction 'once' can be kept directly in front of a reduced passive participle: 'Once it has been translated' becomes 'Once translated', dropping the subject and the auxiliary 'has been'. The understood subject is still 'the manual', the subject of the main clause, so the participle does not dangle.

items.prompt: She spoke quietly. She did not want to wake the baby.
items.instruction: Combine using a negative perfect-less present participle clause of reason placed after the main clause, ending '…, not wanting to…'.
answer: She spoke quietly, not wanting to wake the baby.
She spoke quietly not wanting to wake the baby
items.explanation: An -ing clause after the main clause can give the reason or motive behind it: 'not wanting to wake the baby' explains why she spoke quietly. The negative is formed by placing 'not' before the participle, and the shared subject 'she' is understood as the subject of 'wanting'.

items.prompt: The candidate gave a polished speech. As a result, she impressed the entire panel.
items.instruction: Combine using a present participle clause introduced by 'thereby' to express the deliberate result, ending '…, thereby impressing…'.
answer: The candidate gave a polished speech, thereby impressing the entire panel.
The candidate gave a polished speech thereby impressing the entire panel
items.explanation: A result -ing clause can be reinforced with the formal adverb 'thereby' ('by that means / as a result of that'): 'thereby impressing the entire panel' = 'and by doing so impressed them'. The participle's understood subject is 'the candidate', the main subject, and 'thereby' makes the cause-and-effect link explicit.

items.prompt: The samples were stored at the wrong temperature. They were therefore useless for analysis.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect passive participle clause of reason, beginning 'Having been stored'.
answer: Having been stored at the wrong temperature, the samples were useless for analysis.
Having been stored at the wrong temperature the samples were useless for analysis
items.explanation: When the reason is both passive and prior in time, it reduces to the perfect passive participle 'Having been + past participle': 'Having been stored at the wrong temperature' = 'Because they had been stored…'. The understood subject is 'the samples', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: Anyone who is wishing to apply must submit references by Friday.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ing participle) in place of 'who is wishing', beginning 'Anyone wishing'.
answer: Anyone wishing to apply must submit references by Friday.
Anyone wishing to apply must submit references by Friday
items.explanation: A defining relative clause of the form 'who/that + be + -ing' reduces to the bare present participle: 'who is wishing' becomes 'wishing'. This postmodifying participle ('Anyone wishing to apply') is a reduced relative clause, not an adverbial one, and is standard in formal and official register.

items.prompt: If it is treated promptly, the infection rarely causes lasting damage.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'If' but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle.
answer: If treated promptly, the infection rarely causes lasting damage.
If treated promptly the infection rarely causes lasting damage
items.explanation: A conditional clause can be reduced to a participle while the conjunction is kept for clarity: 'If it is treated promptly' becomes 'If treated promptly', dropping the subject and 'is'. The understood subject is 'the infection', the subject of the main clause, so the reduced conditional does not dangle.

items.prompt: As soon as he arrived at the station, he realised he had forgotten his ticket.
items.instruction: Rewrite the time clause using 'On + -ing', beginning with 'On arriving'.
answer: On arriving at the station, he realised he had forgotten his ticket.
On arriving at the station he realised he had forgotten his ticket
items.explanation: 'On + -ing' expresses an action immediately followed by another: 'On arriving at the station' = 'As soon as he arrived'. The understood subject of the gerund is 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the construction is well formed.

items.prompt: Because she had missed the last train, she was forced to take a taxi home.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a perfect participle clause of reason, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having missed the last train, she was forced to take a taxi home.
Having missed the last train she was forced to take a taxi home
items.explanation: A causal clause referring to an earlier completed action reduces to a perfect participle: 'Because she had missed' becomes 'Having missed', which conveys both the reason and the fact that the action was finished before the main event. The shared subject 'she' is the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: The old factory stood at the edge of town. Its windows were boarded up against intruders.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'with + noun + -ed participle' absolute construction for the attendant state, placed after the main clause (', with its windows…').
answer: The old factory stood at the edge of town, with its windows boarded up against intruders.
The old factory stood at the edge of town with its windows boarded up against intruders
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'With + noun + -ed (passive) participle' describes an attendant state and has its own subject ('its windows'). Because the windows were acted upon (they were boarded up), the past participle is used rather than the -ing form, and the separate subject means the clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: Hoping to find a bargain, the shelves were searched thoroughly by the customer.
items.instruction: Correct the dangling modifier so that the -ing clause attaches to its true subject; keep the opening 'Hoping to find a bargain,'.
answer: Hoping to find a bargain, the customer searched the shelves thoroughly.
Hoping to find a bargain the customer searched the shelves thoroughly
items.placeholder: Hoping to find a bargain, the customer…
items.explanation: In the original, the participle 'Hoping' dangles because its understood subject (the one hoping) is not 'the shelves', the grammatical subject of the passive main clause. The fix makes the human agent 'the customer' the subject of an active main clause, so the participle now correctly attaches to the person who is hoping.

items.prompt: The villa had been abandoned for decades. It was finally restored to its original splendour.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a perfect passive participle clause for the completed prior state, beginning with 'Having been'.
answer: Having been abandoned for decades, the villa was finally restored to its original splendour.
Having been abandoned for decades the villa was finally restored to its original splendour
items.explanation: A passive clause that is also prior in time reduces to the perfect passive participle 'Having been + past participle': 'Having been abandoned for decades' marks the state as both passive (the villa was acted upon) and completed before the restoration. The understood subject is 'the villa', the subject of the main clause, so the participle does not dangle.

items.prompt: The documents were stored in a damp cellar. They had deteriorated beyond repair.
items.instruction: Combine using a past (-ed passive) participle clause for the cause, beginning with 'Stored'.
answer: Stored in a damp cellar, the documents had deteriorated beyond repair.
Stored in a damp cellar the documents had deteriorated beyond repair
items.explanation: A passive clause ('Because they had been stored in a damp cellar') reduces to the bare past participle 'Stored', which here carries a causal sense as well as describing the documents. The understood subject is 'the documents', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier attaches correctly and does not dangle.

items.prompt: The candidates who are shortlisted for the post will be contacted next week.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (a past participle) instead of 'who are shortlisted'.
answer: The candidates shortlisted for the post will be contacted next week.
The candidates shortlisted for the post will be contacted next week
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'who/which + be + past participle' reduces to the bare participle: 'who are shortlisted' becomes 'shortlisted'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the candidates', not an adverbial one, so no commas are added.

items.prompt: Having reviewed all the evidence. The jury reached a unanimous verdict.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a perfect participle clause for the completed first action, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having reviewed all the evidence, the jury reached a unanimous verdict.
Having reviewed all the evidence the jury reached a unanimous verdict
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having reviewed' signals that the reviewing was completed before the verdict was reached, making the sequence explicit. The understood subject of the participle must match the subject of the main clause: it is 'the jury' that did the reviewing, so the shared subject keeps the clause from dangling.

items.prompt: While he was recovering from surgery, the author dictated his final novel.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'While' but reducing the clause to a present (-ing) participle.
answer: While recovering from surgery, the author dictated his final novel.
While recovering from surgery the author dictated his final novel
items.explanation: A subordinating conjunction (while, when, once, before, after) may be kept in front of a participle clause for precision: 'While he was recovering' reduces to 'While recovering', dropping the subject and the form of 'be'. The understood subject remains 'the author', the subject of the main clause, so the reduced clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: Because she lived only a short walk from the office, she rarely took the bus.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a positive present (-ing) participle clause of cause at the front, beginning with 'Living'.
answer: Living only a short walk from the office, she rarely took the bus.
Living only a short walk from the office she rarely took the bus
items.explanation: A 'because'-clause of reason reduces to a fronted -ing participle clause expressing cause: 'Because she lived…' becomes 'Living…'. The participle here does not describe a simultaneous physical action but states the reason for the main clause, a recognised use of the -ing clause (Swan, Practical English Usage, 'participle clauses'). The understood subject is 'she', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier is correctly attached and does not dangle.

items.prompt: Driving to work this morning, the traffic was the worst I had seen in years.
items.instruction: The fronted participle clause dangles because its subject is not the main-clause subject. Rewrite to correct it, keeping 'Driving to work this morning,' and supplying a proper subject ('I').
answer: Driving to work this morning, I saw the worst traffic in years.
Driving to work this morning I saw the worst traffic in years.
Driving to work this morning, I found the traffic the worst I had seen in years.
Driving to work this morning I found the traffic the worst I had seen in years
items.placeholder: Driving to work this morning, I…
items.explanation: The original is a classic dangling (or 'unattached') participle: 'Driving to work' has no stated subject, so by rule it attaches to the main-clause subject 'the traffic' — yet traffic does not drive. The correction makes the human agent 'I' the subject of the main clause, so the understood subject of 'Driving' matches it (Swan, Practical English Usage, 'misrelated/dangling participles'). Any rewrite is acceptable provided the person who was driving is the grammatical subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: The decision had been taken. The board turned to the question of how to announce it.
items.instruction: Combine using an absolute construction of the form 'noun + past participle' at the front (no conjunction, no 'with'), beginning with 'The decision'.
answer: The decision taken, the board turned to the question of how to announce it.
The decision taken the board turned to the question of how to announce it
items.explanation: An absolute construction may consist of a noun plus a past (passive) participle with no introductory word: 'The decision taken' = 'Once/After the decision had been taken'. Unlike an ordinary participle clause, the absolute has its own overt subject ('the decision'), distinct from the main-clause subject ('the board'), so it cannot dangle (Cambridge Grammar of English, 'absolute clauses'). It compresses a completed, passive prior event into a noun + -ed phrase.

items.prompt: After the software had been installed, the technician restarted the machine.
items.instruction: Rewrite reducing the time clause to a past (passive) participle but keeping the conjunction 'Once'.
answer: Once installed, the software prompted the technician to restart the machine.
Once installed the software prompted the technician to restart the machine
Once installed, the software let the technician restart the machine.
Once installed the software let the technician restart the machine
items.explanation: A conjunction of time (once, when, until, after) can be kept before a reduced passive participle: 'Once the software had been installed' becomes 'Once installed'. Because the participle has no subject of its own, the noun it modifies must become the subject of the main clause — here 'the software' — otherwise the clause would dangle (the original main subject 'the technician' was not installed). The rewrite therefore promotes 'the software' to subject. 'Once + past participle' is a common reduced temporal clause (Swan, Practical English Usage).

items.prompt: The path that leads down to the harbour is steep and badly lit.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ing participle) in subject position, replacing 'that leads'.
answer: The path leading down to the harbour is steep and badly lit.
The path leading down to the harbour is steep and badly lit
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with an active verb can be reduced to an -ing participle even when the head noun is the subject of the whole sentence: 'The path that leads down to the harbour' becomes 'The path leading down to the harbour'. The reduction works because 'the path' is the subject of the relative verb 'leads'; the participle 'leading' then post-modifies the subject noun phrase. This is a reduced relative (adjectival) clause, distinct from an adverbial participle clause (Swan, Practical English Usage, 'relative clauses: leaving out the relative pronoun').

items.prompt: Because it stands on a hill, the castle can be seen from miles away.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a fronted causal -ing participle clause, beginning with 'Standing'.
answer: Standing on a hill, the castle can be seen from miles away.
Standing on a hill the castle can be seen from miles away
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because it stands…') reduces to a present participle clause expressing reason: 'Standing on a hill' = 'Because it stands on a hill'. The understood subject is 'the castle', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier is correctly attached and does not dangle.

items.prompt: The weather was fine, so we decided to eat lunch outside on the terrace.
items.instruction: Rewrite the first clause as a bare absolute construction (noun + participle, no 'with'), beginning with 'The weather'.
answer: The weather being fine, we decided to eat lunch outside on the terrace.
The weather being fine we decided to eat lunch outside on the terrace
items.explanation: A bare absolute construction keeps its own subject and links it to the main clause through a participle: 'The weather being fine' has the subject 'the weather', distinct from 'we'. Because the absolute clause carries its own subject, it does not need to share the subject of the main clause and so does not dangle.

items.prompt: Driving to the coast, the sea suddenly came into view.
items.instruction: This sentence contains a dangling modifier (the sea was not driving). Rewrite it so the participle clause has a proper subject, beginning with 'Driving to the coast, we'.
answer: Driving to the coast, we suddenly saw the sea come into view.
Driving to the coast, we suddenly saw the sea.
Driving to the coast we suddenly saw the sea
items.explanation: The original dangles: the understood subject of 'Driving' must be the subject of the main clause, but 'the sea' cannot drive. Supplying a human subject ('we') for the main clause makes 'we' the shared subject of the participle, so the modifier no longer dangles.

items.prompt: He had lost his passport. He was unable to board the flight.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause with an object, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lost his passport, he was unable to board the flight.
Having lost his passport he was unable to board the flight
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having lost his passport' marks the loss as completed before the main event and, here, expresses its cause ('Because he had lost…'). A plain -ing clause ('Losing his passport') would wrongly suggest the two events were simultaneous.

items.prompt: If the weather permits, the ceremony will take place in the garden.
items.instruction: Rewrite the condition as an absolute participle phrase using 'permitting', placed after the main clause.
answer: The ceremony will take place in the garden, weather permitting.
The ceremony will take place in the garden weather permitting
items.explanation: 'Weather permitting' is a fixed absolute construction (noun + -ing participle) meaning 'if the weather permits'. Like other absolutes it carries its own subject ('weather'), distinct from 'the ceremony', so it attaches without a shared subject and does not dangle.

items.prompt: The results had been checked twice. They were sent to the editor.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect passive participle clause for the completed prior action, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having been checked twice, the results were sent to the editor.
Having been checked twice the results were sent to the editor
items.explanation: 'Having been + past participle' reduces a clause that is both passive and prior in time: 'The results had been checked twice' becomes 'Having been checked twice'. The understood subject is 'the results', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The tourists did not have a map. They wandered the old town for hours.
items.instruction: Rewrite the first idea as a negative present participle clause of reason, beginning with 'Not'.
answer: Not having a map, the tourists wandered the old town for hours.
Not having a map the tourists wandered the old town for hours
items.explanation: A reason clause reduces to a participle clause with 'not' placed before the participle: 'Because they did not have a map' becomes 'Not having a map'. The subject 'the tourists' is shared with the main clause.

items.prompt: Once it is opened, the medicine must be used within a month.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'Once' but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle.
answer: Once opened, the medicine must be used within a month.
Once opened the medicine must be used within a month
items.explanation: A subordinating conjunction can be retained in front of a reduced clause for precision: 'Once it is opened' drops the subject and the form of 'be' to give 'Once opened'. The understood subject remains 'the medicine', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: The candidate had lost the previous three elections. She was written off by the pundits.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect (active) participle clause for the completed prior situation, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lost the previous three elections, the candidate was written off by the pundits.
Having lost the previous three elections the candidate was written off by the pundits
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having lost' marks the losses as complete before the main event, in a way a plain -ing clause ('Losing') could not. The shared subject 'the candidate' is understood as the subject of the participle, so the modifier does not dangle even though the main clause is passive.

items.prompt: The storm knocked out the power lines. It left thousands of homes in darkness.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using an -ing participle clause to express the result, ending with a clause beginning '…, leaving…'.
answer: The storm knocked out the power lines, leaving thousands of homes in darkness.
The storm knocked out the power lines leaving thousands of homes in darkness
items.explanation: An -ing clause following the main clause can express a result or consequence: 'leaving thousands of homes in darkness' = 'and this left…'. The understood subject is 'the storm', the subject of the main clause, so no dangling modifier arises.

items.prompt: If you turn left at the old church, you will see the museum directly ahead of you.
items.instruction: Rewrite the conditional clause as a present (-ing) participle clause expressing an open condition, beginning with 'Turning'.
answer: Turning left at the old church, you will see the museum directly ahead of you.
Turning left at the old church you will see the museum directly ahead of you
items.explanation: A present participle clause can express an open (real) condition equivalent to an 'if'-clause: 'Turning left at the old church' = 'If you turn left at the old church'. The understood subject of 'Turning' is 'you', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle. This conditional use is distinct from a merely simultaneous -ing clause because it names the circumstance under which the main clause becomes true.

items.prompt: She calmed the frightened child by singing softly to him.
items.instruction: Rewrite so that the means/manner is expressed by a fronted present (-ing) participle clause, beginning with 'Singing'.
answer: Singing softly to him, she calmed the frightened child.
Singing softly to him she calmed the frightened child
items.explanation: A fronted -ing clause can express the means or manner by which the main action is carried out: 'Singing softly to him' answers 'how' she calmed the child, corresponding to 'by singing softly to him'. Because the singing and the calming are performed by the same subject at the same time and 'she' is understood as the subject of the participle, no separate subject is needed.

items.prompt: He had lost his keys. As a result, he had to climb in through the kitchen window.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause expressing the prior cause, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lost his keys, he had to climb in through the kitchen window.
Having lost his keys he had to climb in through the kitchen window
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having lost' marks an action completed before the main verb and presents it as the cause of what follows: 'Having lost his keys' = 'Because he had lost his keys'. Unlike a plain -ing clause of simultaneous action, the perfect form makes the earlier, causative event explicit, and the understood subject 'he' matches the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: The weather was fine. We set off early for the summit.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction (a noun with a present participle) to give the attendant circumstance, beginning with 'The weather'.
answer: The weather being fine, we set off early for the summit.
The weather being fine we set off early for the summit
items.explanation: In a nominative absolute the participle keeps its own explicit subject: 'The weather was fine' becomes 'The weather being fine', with the finite 'was' turning into the participle 'being'. Because the construction carries its own subject ('the weather'), it does not need to match the main-clause subject ('we') and so does not dangle.

items.prompt: Once it has been signed by both parties, the contract becomes legally binding.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'Once' but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle.
answer: Once signed by both parties, the contract becomes legally binding.
Once signed by both parties the contract becomes legally binding
items.explanation: The subordinating conjunction 'Once' can be kept in front of a reduced passive participle for precision: 'Once it has been signed' loses its subject and the auxiliary 'has been', leaving 'Once signed'. The understood subject remains 'the contract', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: She had not received a reply. She wrote to the company again.
items.instruction: Combine using a negative perfect participle clause to show the prior (non-)event, beginning with 'Not having'.
answer: Not having received a reply, she wrote to the company again.
Not having received a reply she wrote to the company again
items.explanation: The negative perfect participle 'Not having + past participle' combines two features at once: 'having received' marks the action as completed before the main verb, and 'not' negates the whole clause. 'Not having received a reply' = 'Since she had not received a reply'. The negator 'not' is placed before the participle, and the understood subject stays 'she', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The evidence that was gathered during the raid proved crucial at the trial.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced defining relative clause built on a past (passive) participle (drop 'that was').
answer: The evidence gathered during the raid proved crucial at the trial.
The evidence gathered during the raid proved crucial at the trial
items.explanation: A defining relative clause of the form 'that/which + was + past participle' reduces to the bare past participle: 'that was gathered' becomes 'gathered'. Because the clause is defining (it tells us which evidence), no commas are used — unlike the non-defining reduced relative, which keeps its commas. The participle 'gathered' modifies the noun 'the evidence', so this is a reduced relative clause, not an adverbial participle clause.

items.prompt: The travellers were exhausted by the long journey. They fell asleep the moment they sat down.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a fronted past (-ed) participle clause expressing the cause, beginning with 'Exhausted'.
answer: Exhausted by the long journey, they fell asleep the moment they sat down.
Exhausted by the long journey they fell asleep the moment they sat down
items.explanation: A stative/passive reason clause ('Because they were exhausted by the long journey') reduces to the bare past participle 'Exhausted by the long journey', which here expresses cause rather than mere description. The understood subject is 'they', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle. Unlike a reduced relative such as 'Built in 1890', this clause modifies the whole main clause adverbially (explaining why they fell asleep), not just a preceding noun.

items.prompt: She had already read the book twice. She saw no reason to buy another copy.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause expressing the reason, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having already read the book twice, she saw no reason to buy another copy.
Having already read the book twice she saw no reason to buy another copy
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having already read' marks the action as completed before the main verb and, in this context, supplies the reason for it: because she had read the book twice, she saw no reason to buy another. This causal use of 'Having + past participle' differs from the purely temporal sequence in 'Having finished the report, he went home'; the shared subject 'she' keeps the clause from dangling.

items.prompt: Because she lives near the office, she walks to work every day.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present (-ing) participle clause, beginning with 'Living'.
answer: Living near the office, she walks to work every day.
Living near the office she walks to work every day
items.explanation: A causal clause expressing an ongoing state ('Because she lives near the office') reduces to a present participle clause of reason: 'Living near the office'. The understood subject is 'she', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle. Unlike a simultaneous -ing clause, this one answers 'why', not 'when'.

items.prompt: Once it has been opened, the medicine must be used within four weeks.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'Once' but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle.
answer: Once opened, the medicine must be used within four weeks.
Once opened the medicine must be used within four weeks
items.explanation: The conjunction 'Once' can be retained in front of a reduced participle clause for precision: 'Once it has been opened' becomes 'Once opened', dropping the subject and the passive auxiliary 'has been'. The understood subject stays 'the medicine', the subject of the main clause, so the clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: The report was finally finished. She turned her attention to her overflowing inbox.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'with + noun + past participle' absolute construction for the completed circumstance, beginning with 'With'.
answer: With the report finally finished, she turned her attention to her overflowing inbox.
With the report finally finished she turned her attention to her overflowing inbox
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'With + noun + past participle' supplies a completed attendant circumstance and has its own subject ('the report'), so it need not share the subject of the main clause ('she'). The past participle 'finished' carries a passive, resultative sense — 'now that the report had been finished' — distinguishing it from the -ing absolute, which describes an ongoing circumstance.

items.prompt: After he had signed the contract, he immediately regretted it.
items.instruction: Reduce the time clause to a participle clause, keeping the conjunction 'After' and using a simple -ing form, beginning with 'After signing'.
answer: After signing the contract, he immediately regretted it.
After signing the contract he immediately regretted it
items.explanation: A time clause introduced by 'after', 'before', 'since', 'when', 'while' or 'on' can be reduced to a participle clause when its subject matches that of the main clause: 'After he had signed' becomes 'After signing'. Because 'after' itself already marks the action as the earlier of the two, a simple -ing form suffices and the perfect participle ('after having signed') is unnecessary. The understood subject of 'signing' remains 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: Having read the instructions carefully, the machine was easy to assemble.
items.instruction: This sentence has a dangling (misrelated) participle: 'Having read the instructions carefully' cannot refer to 'the machine'. Rewrite it, keeping the participle clause unchanged, but giving the main clause a proper human subject by beginning it with 'I found'.
answer: Having read the instructions carefully, I found the machine easy to assemble.
Having read the instructions carefully I found the machine easy to assemble
items.placeholder: Having read the instructions carefully, I found…
items.explanation: A fronted participle clause takes its subject from the subject of the main clause; when the two do not match, the result is a dangling or misrelated participle. In the original, 'Having read the instructions' wrongly attaches to 'the machine', which cannot read, so a human subject must be supplied: 'I found the machine easy to assemble'. This is why an introductory participle requires the main clause to name the doer of the participle's action.

items.prompt: He had finished his work. He went for a walk.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction with a noun + past participle (no 'having' and no 'with'), beginning 'His work finished, …'.
answer: His work finished, he went for a walk.
His work finished he went for a walk
items.explanation: A nominative absolute (or 'absolute construction') consists of a noun or pronoun followed by a participle and stands grammatically apart from the main clause, carrying its own subject: 'His work finished' means 'When/Once his work had been finished'. Because the absolute supplies its own subject ('his work'), it need not share the subject of the main clause ('he'), and the past participle 'finished' expresses a completed, passive state. Unlike the 'with + noun + -ing' pattern, this bare absolute uses no 'with' and a past participle rather than an -ing form.

items.prompt: Because he was a doctor, he knew at once what the symptoms meant.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present participle clause using the participle of 'be', beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being a doctor, he knew at once what the symptoms meant.
Being a doctor he knew at once what the symptoms meant
items.explanation: A causal clause with the copula ('Because/As he was a doctor') reduces to the present participle of 'be' plus its complement: 'Being a doctor' expresses reason. The understood subject of 'Being' is 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: His work was finished. He left the office and headed straight home.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction (a noun followed by a past participle, with no 'with'), beginning 'His work finished,'.
answer: His work finished, he left the office and headed straight home.
His work finished he left the office and headed straight home
items.explanation: The nominative absolute 'His work finished' (= 'When/Once his work was finished') pairs a noun with a past participle and, crucially, carries its own subject, 'his work'. Because that subject is stated within the absolute rather than borrowed from the main clause, the construction supplies the attendant circumstance without dangling, even though the main subject is the different 'he'.

items.prompt: Once the report has been finalised, it will be circulated to all members.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'Once' but reducing the subordinate clause to a past (passive) participle, making 'the report' the subject of the main clause.
answer: Once finalised, the report will be circulated to all members.
Once finalised the report will be circulated to all members
items.explanation: The conjunction 'Once' can be retained in front of a reduced clause for precision: 'Once it has been finalised' becomes 'Once finalised', dropping the subject and the passive 'has been'. The understood subject must then surface as the subject of the main clause ('the report'), or the modifier would dangle.

items.prompt: Because he had lost his keys, he had to climb in through the window.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a perfect participle clause expressing cause, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lost his keys, he had to climb in through the window.
Having lost his keys he had to climb in through the window
items.explanation: A perfect participle can express cause as well as mere sequence: 'Having lost his keys' = 'Because he had lost his keys'. The 'Having + past participle' form marks the losing as complete and prior, and the shared subject 'he' licenses the reduction.

items.prompt: The road that leads to the village is closed for repairs.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ing participle) in place of 'that leads'.
answer: The road leading to the village is closed for repairs.
The road leading to the village is closed for repairs
items.explanation: A defining relative clause whose verb is a simple active present ('that leads') reduces directly to an -ing participle: 'that leads' becomes 'leading'. Unlike the reduction of 'who is sitting' (be + -ing), here the participle replaces an ordinary finite verb, a pattern reserved for permanent or characterising facts.

items.prompt: He had lost his passport somewhere in the terminal. He was unable to board the flight.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a perfect participle clause to express the reason for the second event, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lost his passport, he was unable to board the flight.
Having lost his passport he was unable to board the flight
Having lost his passport somewhere in the terminal, he was unable to board the flight.
Having lost his passport somewhere in the terminal he was unable to board the flight
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having lost' here expresses cause rather than mere sequence: 'Having lost his passport' means 'Because he had lost his passport'. The same 'Having + past participle' form that can mark a completed prior action also serves to give the reason for the main clause, and the understood subject stays 'he', the subject of the main clause, so nothing dangles.

items.prompt: The path that leads to the village winds steeply through dense forest.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ing participle) in place of 'that leads'.
answer: The path leading to the village winds steeply through dense forest.
The path leading to the village winds steeply through dense forest
items.explanation: A defining relative clause whose verb is in the simple present ('that leads') can still be reduced to an -ing participle: 'that leads' becomes 'leading', even though the original is not a progressive 'is leading'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the path', so the participle stands in for the whole finite verb and its understood subject is 'the path', the noun it describes.

items.prompt: He drove along the coast road. He admired the spectacular view.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a present (-ing) participle clause for the simultaneous action, beginning with 'Driving'.
answer: Driving along the coast road, he admired the spectacular view.
Driving along the coast road he admired the spectacular view
items.explanation: Two simultaneous actions by the same subject combine with an -ing clause: 'Driving along the coast road' replaces 'While he was driving along the coast road'. The subject 'he' is shared by both clauses, so the participle does not dangle.

items.prompt: She had spent twenty years abroad. She found her home town strangely unfamiliar.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause to show the first situation preceded the second, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having spent twenty years abroad, she found her home town strangely unfamiliar.
Having spent twenty years abroad she found her home town strangely unfamiliar
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having spent' marks the twenty years abroad as completed before the moment of finding the town unfamiliar. A plain -ing clause ('Spending twenty years abroad') would wrongly imply the two were simultaneous; the shared subject 'she' keeps the clause from dangling.

items.prompt: The palace was destroyed by fire in 1698. It was never rebuilt.
items.instruction: Combine using an -ed (passive) participle clause, beginning with 'Destroyed'.
answer: Destroyed by fire in 1698, the palace was never rebuilt.
Destroyed by fire in 1698 the palace was never rebuilt
items.explanation: A passive clause ('which was destroyed by fire in 1698') reduces to the bare past participle 'Destroyed by fire in 1698'. The understood subject is 'the palace', the subject of the main clause, so the fronted participle does not dangle.

items.prompt: The documents that were seized in the raid have still not been returned.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (a past participle) in place of 'that were seized'.
answer: The documents seized in the raid have still not been returned.
The documents seized in the raid have still not been returned
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'that/which + be + past participle' reduces to the bare past participle: 'that were seized' becomes 'seized'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the documents', not an adverbial one, so no commas are used and the participle sits directly after the noun it defines.

items.prompt: Because he lacked any formal training, he taught himself to paint.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present participle clause, beginning with 'Lacking'.
answer: Lacking any formal training, he taught himself to paint.
Lacking any formal training he taught himself to paint
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because he lacked any formal training') reduces to a present participle clause of reason: 'Lacking any formal training'. The stative verb 'lack' takes the -ing form even though it denotes no continuous action, and the shared subject 'he' prevents the modifier from dangling.

items.prompt: Because they lived far from the nearest town, they rarely received visitors.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present (-ing) participle clause at the front, beginning with 'Living'.
answer: Living far from the nearest town, they rarely received visitors.
Living far from the nearest town they rarely received visitors
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because they lived far from the nearest town') reduces to a fronted -ing clause of reason: 'Living far from the nearest town'. The conjunction and the subject are dropped, and the finite verb 'lived' becomes the participle 'living'. Because the understood subject is 'they', the subject of the main clause, the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: As soon as she arrived at the office, she realised she had left her laptop at home.
items.instruction: Rewrite the time clause using 'On' + an -ing participle to mean 'immediately after', beginning with 'On'.
answer: On arriving at the office, she realised she had left her laptop at home.
On arriving at the office she realised she had left her laptop at home
items.explanation: The pattern 'On/Upon + -ing' reduces a time clause and means 'immediately after' the action: 'As soon as she arrived' becomes 'On arriving'. Unlike a conjunction kept in front of a past participle ('When questioned'), the preposition 'on' governs a gerund-participle here. The understood subject stays 'she', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: He listened to the whole speech. His arms were folded the entire time.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'with + noun + past participle' absolute construction for the accompanying state, ending with ', with his arms folded'.
answer: He listened to the whole speech, with his arms folded.
He listened to the whole speech with his arms folded
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'With + noun + past participle' describes an accompanying state passively: 'his arms were folded' becomes 'with his arms folded'. Like the -ing absolute, it carries its own subject ('his arms'), so it need not share the subject of the main clause ('he') and does not dangle; the past participle marks the state as passive rather than an ongoing action.

items.prompt: He had never travelled abroad before. He found the airport's noise and bustle overwhelming.
items.instruction: Combine using a negative perfect participle clause for the prior lack of experience, beginning with 'Never having'.
answer: Never having travelled abroad before, he found the airport's noise and bustle overwhelming.
Never having travelled abroad before he found the airport's noise and bustle overwhelming
items.explanation: A perfect participle ('Having travelled') reduces a prior completed situation; a perfect participle is negated by placing the negator before it, and 'never' is fronted for emphasis, giving 'Never having travelled abroad' = 'As he had never travelled abroad'. The understood subject is 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the participle does not dangle. This differs from the plain negative present participle 'Not knowing', because the perfect form marks the experience as completed (or, here, never accomplished) before the main verb.

items.prompt: The weather was fine. We decided to set out at dawn.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction (a participle clause that keeps its own subject and uses no 'with'), beginning with 'The weather being'.
answer: The weather being fine, we decided to set out at dawn.
The weather being fine we decided to set out at dawn
items.explanation: The nominative absolute 'The weather being fine' consists of a subject ('the weather') plus a participle ('being'), forming a clause that is grammatically independent of the main clause. Because it carries its own explicit subject, it need not share the main-clause subject 'we' and therefore cannot dangle. It expresses the attendant circumstance by the same logic as the 'with + noun + -ing' absolute, but without the introductory 'with'.

items.prompt: The chairman's speech had ended. The delegates filed out of the hall.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction (with its OWN subject and a perfect participle, and no 'with'), beginning with 'The chairman's speech having ended, …'.
answer: The chairman's speech having ended, the delegates filed out of the hall.
The chairman's speech having ended the delegates filed out of the hall
items.explanation: A nominative (or free) absolute construction keeps its own overt subject — 'The chairman's speech' — followed by a participle, and stands grammatically independent of the main clause. Because it supplies its own subject, it does not need to share the subject of the main clause and cannot dangle: the delegates are not the ones ending. The perfect participle 'having ended' marks the speech as finished before they filed out; the whole phrase is set off by a comma and reads like a compressed 'When/After the chairman's speech had ended'.

items.prompt: If you turn left at the second set of traffic lights, you will see the museum on your right.
items.instruction: Rewrite the conditional 'if' clause as a present (-ing) participle clause expressing the condition, beginning with 'Turning'.
answer: Turning left at the second set of traffic lights, you will see the museum on your right.
Turning left at the second set of traffic lights you will see the museum on your right
items.explanation: An open conditional ('If you turn left…') can be reduced to a present participle clause when its subject is the same as that of the main clause: 'Turning left' = 'If you turn left'. Here the -ing clause expresses a condition rather than a simultaneous action or reason — the fulfilment of 'turning left' is the circumstance under which 'you will see the museum' holds. The understood subject is 'you', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The weather was fine. We decided to walk to the coast.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using an absolute (nominative) participle construction that keeps its own subject, beginning with 'The weather being fine'.
answer: The weather being fine, we decided to walk to the coast.
The weather being fine we decided to walk to the coast
items.explanation: This is a nominative absolute construction: 'The weather being fine' has its own overt subject ('the weather') plus the participle 'being', and is only loosely attached to the main clause, supplying a reason or attendant circumstance ('since/as the weather was fine'). Because the participle carries its own subject, it need not share the subject of the main clause, and so, unlike an ordinary -ing clause, it cannot dangle even though its subject ('the weather') differs from the main subject ('we').

items.prompt: Because she was a qualified physician, she knew exactly what to do.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present (-ing) participle clause of cause, beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being a qualified physician, she knew exactly what to do.
Being a qualified physician she knew exactly what to do
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because she was a qualified physician') reduces to the participle of stative 'be' plus its complement: 'Being a qualified physician'. Here the -ing clause expresses reason or circumstance rather than a simultaneous action, which is why the normally dynamic '-ing' can attach to the state verb 'be'. The understood subject of 'Being' must be the subject of the main clause ('she'); were the main subject different, the sentence would dangle.

items.prompt: He had lost all his savings in the crash. He was forced to sell the family home.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause that expresses the cause of the second event, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lost all his savings in the crash, he was forced to sell the family home.
Having lost all his savings in the crash he was forced to sell the family home
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having lost' does two things at once: it marks the action as completed before the main verb, and, because it precedes an unavoidable consequence, it is read as its cause ('Because/Since he had lost all his savings'). This causal reading is what distinguishes it from a merely sequential 'Having finished..., he went home'. The shared subject 'he' is understood as the subject of the participle, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: Because he lived so far from the town centre, he rarely went out in the evenings.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present (-ing) participle clause expressing cause, beginning with 'Living'.
answer: Living so far from the town centre, he rarely went out in the evenings.
Living so far from the town centre he rarely went out in the evenings
items.explanation: A causal clause built on a stative verb ('Because he lived so far…') reduces to an -ing participle that expresses reason rather than a simultaneous action: 'Living so far from the town centre' = 'Because he lived so far…'. Even though 'live' names a state and not an event, the -ing form is still used, and the understood subject stays 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the fronted modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: After she had locked all the doors, she went upstairs to bed.
items.instruction: Rewrite reducing the time clause to a participle after the conjunction 'After', dropping the subject and 'had'.
answer: After locking all the doors, she went upstairs to bed.
After locking all the doors she went upstairs to bed
items.explanation: A time clause introduced by 'after' (also 'before', 'since', 'on' or 'while') can be reduced to conjunction + -ing form: 'After she had locked all the doors' becomes 'After locking all the doors'. Here the participle follows the conjunction directly as an active -ing form, unlike the passive 'When questioned', and because 'after' already signals that one action precedes the other, a plain -ing is enough without the perfect 'having locked'. The understood subject remains 'she', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: After she had submitted her doctoral thesis, she finally took a long holiday.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a perfect participle clause to show the first action was completed before the second, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having submitted her doctoral thesis, she finally took a long holiday.
Having submitted her doctoral thesis she finally took a long holiday
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having + past participle' marks anteriority: the submission was completed before the holiday began, replacing 'After she had submitted'. The understood subject is 'she', the subject of the main clause, so the clause does not dangle.
