items.prompt: The paintings that were discovered in the attic turned out to be clever forgeries.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause built on a past (passive) participle, dropping 'that were'.
answer: The paintings discovered in the attic turned out to be clever forgeries.
The paintings discovered in the attic turned out to be clever forgeries
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'that/which + be + past participle' reduces to the bare past participle: 'that were discovered' becomes 'discovered'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the paintings', not an adverbial one, and because it is defining no commas are used.

items.prompt: Because it stands on a steep hill, the castle is visible for miles around.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present (-ing) participle clause, beginning with 'Standing'.
answer: Standing on a steep hill, the castle is visible for miles around.
Standing on a steep hill the castle is visible for miles around
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because it stands on a hill') reduces to an -ing participle clause expressing reason: 'Standing on a steep hill'. The understood subject of the participle is 'the castle', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: She was sitting by the window. She watched the storm roll in.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a present (-ing) participle clause for the simultaneous action, beginning with 'Sitting'.
answer: Sitting by the window, she watched the storm roll in.
Sitting by the window she watched the storm roll in
items.explanation: Two actions performed by the same subject at the same time collapse into an -ing clause: 'Sitting by the window' replaces 'While she was sitting by the window'. The shared subject 'she' is not repeated, so the participle does not dangle.

items.prompt: They had locked all the doors. Then they left for the airport.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause to show the first action was completed first, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having locked all the doors, they left for the airport.
Having locked all the doors they left for the airport
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having locked' makes explicit that the locking was finished before they left; a plain -ing clause ('Locking all the doors') would wrongly suggest the two actions overlapped rather than one preceding the other.

items.prompt: The statue was carved from a single block of marble. It has survived for two thousand years.
items.instruction: Combine using an -ed (passive) participle clause, beginning with 'Carved'.
answer: Carved from a single block of marble, the statue has survived for two thousand years.
Carved from a single block of marble the statue has survived for two thousand years
items.explanation: A passive clause ('which was carved from a single block of marble') reduces to the bare past participle 'Carved…'. The understood subject is 'the statue', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier attaches correctly and does not dangle.

items.prompt: The candidates who were shortlisted for the post will be interviewed next week.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ed participle) instead of 'who were shortlisted'.
answer: The candidates shortlisted for the post will be interviewed next week.
The candidates shortlisted for the post will be interviewed next week
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'who/which + be + past participle' reduces to the bare past participle: 'who were shortlisted' becomes 'shortlisted'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the candidates', so no commas are added.

items.prompt: Because she lived only a short walk from the office, she rarely bothered with the bus.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present (-ing) participle clause, beginning with 'Living'.
answer: Living only a short walk from the office, she rarely bothered with the bus.
Living only a short walk from the office she rarely bothered with the bus
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because she lived…') reduces to a present participle clause expressing reason: 'Living only a short walk from the office' = 'Because/Since she lived…'. The subject 'she' is shared with the main clause, so the participle does not dangle; a present participle is used because the state is contemporaneous with the main verb, not prior to it.

items.prompt: She had locked every door and window. Only then did she feel able to relax.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause to show the locking was completed first, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having locked every door and window, she finally felt able to relax.
Having locked every door and window she finally felt able to relax
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having locked' marks the securing of the house as complete before the main event, capturing the 'only then' sequence. A plain -ing clause ('Locking every door and window') would wrongly suggest the two happened at once rather than one after the other.

items.prompt: The novel was translated into more than thirty languages. It brought its author sudden international fame.
items.instruction: Combine using an -ed (passive) participle clause, beginning with 'Translated'.
answer: Translated into more than thirty languages, the novel brought its author sudden international fame.
Translated into more than thirty languages the novel brought its author sudden international fame
items.explanation: A passive clause ('which was translated into more than thirty languages') reduces to the bare past participle 'Translated'. The understood subject is 'the novel', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle; the -ed form carries the passive meaning that the novel was translated, not that it translated anything.

items.prompt: The train which is arriving at platform four is the delayed express to Edinburgh.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ing participle) instead of 'which is arriving'.
answer: The train arriving at platform four is the delayed express to Edinburgh.
The train arriving at platform four is the delayed express to Edinburgh
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'which/who + be + -ing' can be reduced to the bare participle: 'which is arriving' becomes 'arriving'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the train', not an adverbial one, so no comma is introduced and the active -ing form is kept because the train is doing the arriving.

items.prompt: The documents that were leaked to the press have forced the company onto the defensive.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause built on a past (passive) participle (drop 'that were').
answer: The documents leaked to the press have forced the company onto the defensive.
The documents leaked to the press have forced the company onto the defensive
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'that/which + be + past participle' reduces to the bare past participle: 'that were leaked' becomes 'leaked'. This reduced relative modifies 'the documents' and, being defining, takes no commas; the -ed form preserves the passive sense that the documents were leaked by someone.

items.prompt: He was the youngest of five children. He was often overlooked at family gatherings.
items.instruction: Combine using a present participle clause expressing reason (cause), reducing 'be' to its participle and beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being the youngest of five children, he was often overlooked at family gatherings.
Being the youngest of five children he was often overlooked at family gatherings
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because he was the youngest') reduces to an -ing clause built on the copula: 'be' becomes 'Being'. Unlike a simultaneous -ing clause, this one gives the reason for the main clause, and the understood subject 'he' is shared with the main clause, so it does not dangle.

items.prompt: The soldiers stood rigidly to attention. Their eyes were fixed on the horizon.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'with + noun + past participle' absolute construction to give the attendant circumstances, placed after the main clause (', with their eyes…').
answer: The soldiers stood rigidly to attention, with their eyes fixed on the horizon.
The soldiers stood rigidly to attention with their eyes fixed on the horizon
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'With + noun + past participle' supplies attendant circumstances from a passive idea ('their eyes were fixed'): the form of 'be' is dropped, leaving the bare past participle 'fixed'. Because the construction carries its own subject ('their eyes'), it does not need to share the subject of the main clause and so does not dangle.

items.prompt: He realised that he was running dangerously late. He hailed the first taxi he saw.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a present (-ing) participle clause for the reason, beginning with 'Realising'.
answer: Realising he was running dangerously late, he hailed the first taxi he saw.
Realising he was running dangerously late he hailed the first taxi he saw
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because he realised…') reduces to a present participle clause of reason: 'Realising he was running dangerously late' means 'Since he realised he was running late'. The understood subject of 'Realising' is 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier attaches to the right noun and does not dangle.

items.prompt: The researchers had gathered enough evidence. They began to draft their conclusions.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause to show the first action was completed first, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having gathered enough evidence, the researchers began to draft their conclusions.
Having gathered enough evidence the researchers began to draft their conclusions
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having gathered' makes explicit that the evidence had been collected before the drafting started, a sequence a plain -ing clause ('Gathering enough evidence') would blur. The shared subject 'the researchers' is named once in the main clause, so the participle is not left dangling.

items.prompt: The paintings were discovered in a dusty attic. They fetched millions at auction.
items.instruction: Combine using an -ed (passive) participle clause, beginning with 'Discovered'.
answer: Discovered in a dusty attic, the paintings fetched millions at auction.
Discovered in a dusty attic the paintings fetched millions at auction
items.explanation: A passive clause ('which were discovered in a dusty attic') reduces to the past participle alone: 'Discovered in a dusty attic'. The understood subject is 'the paintings', the subject of the main clause, so the participle modifies the right noun and does not dangle.

items.prompt: While he was waiting for the bus, he skimmed through the morning paper.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'While' but reducing the time clause to a present (-ing) participle.
answer: While waiting for the bus, he skimmed through the morning paper.
While waiting for the bus he skimmed through the morning paper
items.explanation: A subordinating conjunction (while, before, after, since) may be kept in front of an -ing participle: 'While he was waiting' reduces to 'While waiting', dropping the subject and the form of 'be'. The understood subject stays 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the clause does not dangle. Unlike a bare -ing clause, the retained conjunction makes the temporal relation explicit.

items.prompt: As soon as she saw the exam results, she phoned her parents.
items.instruction: Rewrite the time clause as an 'On + -ing' participle clause meaning 'as soon as / the moment that', beginning with 'On' (or 'Upon').
answer: On seeing the exam results, she phoned her parents.
On seeing the exam results she phoned her parents
Upon seeing the exam results, she phoned her parents.
Upon seeing the exam results she phoned her parents
items.explanation: 'On (or Upon) + -ing' reduces an 'as soon as / the moment that' clause and stresses that the second action followed immediately: 'On seeing the results' = 'As soon as she saw the results'. The understood subject is 'she', shared with the main clause. Unlike a plain simultaneous -ing clause, 'On + -ing' specifically marks immediate temporal succession rather than overlap.

items.prompt: The weather was fine, so we decided to walk to the coast.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction (its own subject + an -ing participle, with no 'with' and no conjunction), beginning with 'The weather'.
answer: The weather being fine, we decided to walk to the coast.
The weather being fine we decided to walk to the coast
items.explanation: The nominative (or 'absolute') construction 'noun + -ing participle' carries its own subject ('the weather'), distinct from the main-clause subject ('we'), so it does not dangle even though the subjects differ. 'The weather being fine' reduces 'As/Because the weather was fine' and supplies the reason or attendant circumstance. Unlike the 'with + noun + participle' pattern, the bare absolute takes no introductory 'with'.

items.prompt: Because he was a newcomer to the village, he was unfamiliar with its unwritten rules.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a participle clause built on 'being', beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being a newcomer to the village, he was unfamiliar with its unwritten rules.
Being a newcomer to the village he was unfamiliar with its unwritten rules
items.explanation: A clause of the form 'Because + subject + be + complement' reduces to 'Being + complement': 'Because he was a newcomer' becomes 'Being a newcomer'. The -ing participle of 'be' carries the causal sense, and the understood subject remains 'he', the subject of the main clause. Because it reduces a stative be-clause rather than an action, no perfect or passive alternative is available here.

items.prompt: She had not received any reply to her first letter. She therefore wrote again.
items.instruction: Combine using a negative perfect participle clause to show the prior circumstance, beginning with 'Not having'.
answer: Not having received any reply to her first letter, she wrote again.
Not having received any reply to her first letter she wrote again
Not having received any reply to her first letter, she therefore wrote again.
Not having received any reply to her first letter she therefore wrote again
items.explanation: The negative perfect participle 'Not having + past participle' combines anteriority with negation: 'Not having received any reply' = 'Because she had not received any reply'. The perfect form ('having received') is required because the non-receipt was already established before she wrote again, and 'not' is placed before 'having' to negate the whole participle. The understood subject is 'she', shared with the main clause.

items.prompt: She had lived abroad for many years. She found the local customs easy to adapt to.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause expressing the reason, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lived abroad for many years, she found the local customs easy to adapt to.
Having lived abroad for many years she found the local customs easy to adapt to
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having lived' reduces the prior clause and here expresses cause or reason ('Because she had lived abroad'), not mere sequence: her long residence abroad is offered as the explanation for the main clause. The form marks the experience as completed and relevant, and the shared subject 'she' lets the participle stand with no subject of its own.

items.prompt: Once it has been switched on, the device calibrates itself automatically.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'Once' but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle.
answer: Once switched on, the device calibrates itself automatically.
Once switched on the device calibrates itself automatically
items.explanation: A subordinating conjunction other than 'when' can be retained in front of a reduced participle clause for precision: 'Once it has been switched on' reduces to 'Once switched on', dropping the subject and the form of 'be'. The understood subject remains 'the device', the subject of the main clause, so the reduced clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: Walking through the door, the smell of fresh bread greeted us.
items.instruction: This participle clause dangles: the smell was not walking through the door. Rewrite it so the understood subject of 'walking' matches the main clause, beginning with 'Walking through the door, we'.
answer: Walking through the door, we were greeted by the smell of fresh bread.
Walking through the door we were greeted by the smell of fresh bread
items.explanation: The understood subject of a participle clause must be the subject of the main clause. In the original the main subject is 'the smell', which implies the smell walked in — a dangling modifier. Recasting the main clause in the passive so that 'we' becomes its subject ('we were greeted') makes 'we' the doer of 'walking', repairing the broken link.

items.prompt: His work was finished. He allowed himself a long walk by the river.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction (noun + past participle, with no 'with' and no conjunction), beginning with 'His work'.
answer: His work finished, he allowed himself a long walk by the river.
His work finished he allowed himself a long walk by the river
items.explanation: The nominative absolute 'His work finished' pairs a noun ('his work') with a past participle ('finished') and carries its own subject, so it need not share the subject of the main clause ('he'). It supplies the circumstances ('once his work was finished') with no conjunction and no form of 'be' — unlike the 'with + noun + participle' variant, which adds 'with'.

items.prompt: The house, which is being renovated at the moment, has been in the family for generations.
items.instruction: Reduce the relative clause to a participle clause using 'being' (drop 'which is').
answer: The house, being renovated at the moment, has been in the family for generations.
The house being renovated at the moment has been in the family for generations
items.explanation: A relative clause containing a progressive passive ('which is being renovated') reduces by dropping the relative pronoun and the finite 'is', leaving the participle 'being renovated'. 'Being' is the -ing participle of 'be' and carries the passive-progressive sense here; the reduced clause modifies 'the house' and keeps the commas of the non-defining original.

items.prompt: He realized he was already late. He sprinted for the last train.
items.instruction: Combine using a present (-ing) participle clause expressing the reason, beginning with 'Realizing'.
answer: Realizing he was already late, he sprinted for the last train.
Realizing he was already late he sprinted for the last train
items.explanation: A present participle clause can express reason or cause as well as simultaneous action: 'Realizing he was already late' = 'Because he realized he was already late'. The understood subject is 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the participle attaches correctly and does not dangle.

items.prompt: The path that winds up to the summit becomes treacherous after heavy rain.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (an -ing participle) in place of 'that winds', keeping the rest of the sentence unchanged.
answer: The path winding up to the summit becomes treacherous after heavy rain.
The path winding up to the summit becomes treacherous after heavy rain
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with an active verb ('that/which winds') reduces to a bare -ing participle: 'that winds up to the summit' becomes 'winding up to the summit'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the path', not an adverbial clause, so no comma is added.

items.prompt: He had lost everything in the market crash. He was forced to rebuild his career from nothing.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause expressing the reason for the second event, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lost everything in the market crash, he was forced to rebuild his career from nothing.
Having lost everything in the market crash he was forced to rebuild his career from nothing
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having lost' here expresses reason as well as prior time: it reduces 'Because he had lost everything' to a participle clause. The completed sense of 'Having + past participle' shows the loss preceded and caused the situation in the main clause. The understood subject is 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The cathedral, which dominates the old town, attracts thousands of visitors each summer.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced non-defining relative clause with a present (-ing) participle instead of 'which dominates', keeping the commas.
answer: The cathedral, dominating the old town, attracts thousands of visitors each summer.
The cathedral dominating the old town attracts thousands of visitors each summer
items.explanation: A non-defining relative clause with an active verb ('which dominates') can be reduced to a present participle even when there is no form of 'be' to drop: 'which dominates' becomes 'dominating'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the cathedral', not an adverbial one, so the commas of the original non-defining clause are retained.

items.prompt: Once it is exposed to sunlight, the compound loses its distinctive colour within minutes.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'Once' but reducing the clause to a past (passive) participle.
answer: Once exposed to sunlight, the compound loses its distinctive colour within minutes.
Once exposed to sunlight the compound loses its distinctive colour within minutes
items.explanation: The subordinating conjunction 'once' can be kept in front of a reduced participle clause for precision: 'Once it is exposed' reduces to 'Once exposed', dropping the subject and the form of 'be'. The understood subject remains 'the compound', the subject of the main clause, so the clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: As he was a meticulous planner, he had already anticipated every possible objection.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present participle clause of reason at the front, beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being a meticulous planner, he had already anticipated every possible objection.
Being a meticulous planner he had already anticipated every possible objection
items.explanation: A causal clause built on the verb 'be' ('As he was a meticulous planner') reduces to the present participle 'Being': 'Being a meticulous planner' expresses the reason for the main clause. The understood subject is 'he', the subject of the main clause, so no dangling modifier results.

items.prompt: His work was finished at last. He allowed himself a rare evening off.
items.instruction: Combine using an absolute construction WITHOUT 'with' — a noun phrase followed by a past participle — placed at the front, beginning with 'His work'.
answer: His work finished at last, he allowed himself a rare evening off.
His work finished at last he allowed himself a rare evening off
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'noun phrase + past participle' ('His work finished') carries its own subject ('his work'), which is different from the main-clause subject ('he'). Because it supplies that subject itself, it is not a dangling modifier even though its subject differs from that of the main clause; it means 'His work being finished / Now that his work was finished'.

items.prompt: Because he had already seen the exhibition, he offered to be our guide.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a perfect participle clause, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having already seen the exhibition, he offered to be our guide.
Having already seen the exhibition he offered to be our guide
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having + past participle' expresses not only sequence but also REASON: 'Having already seen the exhibition' = 'Because he had already seen it'. The completed prior action supplies the motive for the main verb, and because the understood subject is 'he', the subject of the main clause, the clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: Turning the corner, the old station suddenly came into view.
items.instruction: This sentence has a dangling modifier — the station did not turn the corner. Correct it so that 'we' governs both actions, beginning 'Turning the corner, we saw…'.
answer: Turning the corner, we saw the old station come into view.
Turning the corner we saw the old station come into view
items.explanation: A participle clause dangles when its implied subject is not the subject of the main clause. As originally written, 'Turning' attaches to 'the old station', absurdly implying the station turned the corner. Restoring the real subject 'we' to the main clause makes 'Turning the corner' modify the people who actually turned it, so the modifier no longer dangles.

items.prompt: The weather was fine, so we decided to have lunch in the garden.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction — noun + the present participle of 'be' — to give the reason, beginning 'The weather being fine'.
answer: The weather being fine, we decided to have lunch in the garden.
The weather being fine we decided to have lunch in the garden
items.explanation: The nominative absolute 'The weather being fine' has its own subject ('the weather') and, like the 'with + noun + participle' construction, therefore does not need to share the subject of the main clause. Built on the present participle of 'be', it supplies the attendant circumstance or reason ('since the weather was fine') without any conjunction, and so does not dangle despite its subject differing from 'we'.

items.prompt: The weather was fine. We set out early the next morning.
items.instruction: Combine using a bare absolute construction (a noun + present participle, with no 'with' and no conjunction) to give the attendant circumstance, beginning with 'The weather being'.
answer: The weather being fine, we set out early the next morning.
The weather being fine we set out early the next morning
items.explanation: A nominative absolute has its own overt subject and a participle, standing outside the main clause: 'The weather being fine' = 'Since/As the weather was fine'. Unlike an ordinary participle clause, its subject ('the weather') is different from the subject of the main clause ('we'), yet it does not dangle precisely because that subject is stated. No 'with' is needed; the bare absolute belongs to a formal, slightly literary register.

items.prompt: He had spent all his savings on the failed venture. He could not afford to travel that summer.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'having + past participle' clause to express the reason for the main clause, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having spent all his savings on the failed venture, he could not afford to travel that summer.
Having spent all his savings on the failed venture he could not afford to travel that summer
items.explanation: Here the perfect participle 'Having spent' expresses cause rather than mere sequence: 'Having spent all his savings' = 'Because he had spent all his savings'. The perfect form marks the spending as completed before the main situation, and the shared subject 'he' is understood, so the reason clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: The tourists did not speak the local language. They found it hard to ask for directions.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a present (-ing) participle clause expressing the reason, beginning with 'Not'.
answer: Not speaking the local language, the tourists found it hard to ask for directions.
Not speaking the local language the tourists found it hard to ask for directions
items.explanation: A present participle can replace a reason clause, so 'Because they did not speak the local language' reduces to 'Not speaking the local language'. The negative particle stays in front of the -ing form, and the understood subject of the participle is 'the tourists', the subject of the main clause, so there is no dangling modifier.

items.prompt: The candidate had been rejected twice before. She approached the third interview with little confidence.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a perfect passive participle clause, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having been rejected twice before, the candidate approached the third interview with little confidence.
Having been rejected twice before the candidate approached the third interview with little confidence
items.explanation: The 'Having been + past participle' form is the perfect passive participle, reducing 'After she had been rejected twice before' to 'Having been rejected twice before'. It marks an action completed before the main clause and preserves the passive meaning, with 'the candidate' understood as the subject of both clauses.

items.prompt: The documents were signed by both parties. They were then lodged with the court.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a past (-ed) participle clause to reduce the passive relative clause, beginning with 'Signed'.
answer: Signed by both parties, the documents were then lodged with the court.
Signed by both parties the documents were then lodged with the court
items.explanation: A past participle reduces a passive relative clause, so 'The documents, which had been signed by both parties' becomes 'Signed by both parties'. The -ed participle carries passive meaning on its own, and 'the documents' remains the shared subject of the main clause, avoiding any dangling modifier.

items.prompt: The weather was fine. We set off early for the coast.
items.instruction: Combine the two ideas using a nominative absolute construction (noun + present participle, WITHOUT 'with'), beginning with 'The'.
answer: The weather being fine, we set off early for the coast.
The weather being fine we set off early for the coast
items.explanation: Unlike an ordinary participle clause, an absolute construction keeps its own subject ('the weather'), which differs from the main-clause subject ('we'). The participle 'being' links the two, expressing reason or attendant circumstance ('Since the weather was fine…'). Because the absolute supplies its own subject, there is no dangling modifier.

items.prompt: As soon as she arrived at the office, she realised she had left the documents at home.
items.instruction: Reduce the time clause to an 'On + -ing' participle phrase of immediate sequence, beginning with 'On'.
answer: On arriving at the office, she realised she had left the documents at home.
On arriving at the office she realised she had left the documents at home
On arriving at the office, she realised that she had left the documents at home.
On arriving at the office she realised that she had left the documents at home
items.explanation: 'On/Upon + -ing' reduces a clause of immediate time ('as soon as / the moment that…'), stressing that the second event followed at once. The understood subject of 'arriving' is 'she', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The novel was translated into more than forty languages. It became a worldwide phenomenon.
items.instruction: Combine using a past (-ed) passive participle clause that reduces the passive clause, beginning with 'Translated'.
answer: Translated into more than forty languages, the novel became a worldwide phenomenon.
Translated into more than forty languages the novel became a worldwide phenomenon
items.explanation: A passive clause ('which was translated into more than forty languages') reduces to the bare past participle 'Translated into more than forty languages'. The understood subject of the participle is 'the novel', which is also the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The jury had weighed all the evidence carefully. It then returned a unanimous verdict.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause to show the first action was completed before the second, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having weighed all the evidence carefully, the jury returned a unanimous verdict.
Having weighed all the evidence carefully the jury returned a unanimous verdict
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having weighed' makes explicit that the deliberation was completed before the verdict was returned, a sequence a plain -ing clause ('Weighing the evidence') would blur into simultaneity. The shared subject 'the jury' is the subject of both the participle and the main clause, so there is no dangling modifier.

items.prompt: Once it had been translated into English, the novel reached a much wider audience.
items.instruction: Rewrite keeping the conjunction 'Once' but reducing the passive clause to a past participle (drop 'it had been').
answer: Once translated into English, the novel reached a much wider audience.
Once translated into English the novel reached a much wider audience
items.explanation: A subordinating conjunction can be retained in front of a reduced participle clause for precision: the passive 'Once it had been translated' reduces to 'Once translated', dropping the subject and 'had been'. The understood subject stays 'the novel', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: She had not read the instructions. She assembled the cabinet incorrectly.
items.instruction: Combine using a NEGATIVE perfect participle clause to show the omission preceded the result, beginning with 'Not having'.
answer: Not having read the instructions, she assembled the cabinet incorrectly.
Not having read the instructions she assembled the cabinet incorrectly
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'having read' marks the reading as prior to the assembling, while the negative is formed by placing 'not' before the participle: 'Not having read'. This combines negation and anteriority in one clause, expressing the reason ('because she had not read').

items.prompt: All the preparations had been completed. The team waited nervously for the launch.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'With + noun + past participle' absolute construction for the attendant circumstances, beginning with 'With'.
answer: With all the preparations completed, the team waited nervously for the launch.
With all the preparations completed the team waited nervously for the launch
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'With + noun + past participle' supplies attendant circumstances and carries its own subject ('all the preparations'), so it need not share the main subject ('the team'). The past (passive) participle 'completed' reduces 'which had been completed', so the phrase does not dangle despite the differing subject.

items.prompt: Any student who is found using a phone during the exam will be disqualified.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced DEFINING relative clause built on a past participle, dropping 'who is' (keep it defining, so no commas).
answer: Any student found using a phone during the exam will be disqualified.
Any student found using a phone during the exam will be disqualified
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'who/which + be + past participle' reduces to the bare past participle: 'who is found' becomes 'found'. Because the clause is defining (it identifies which students), no commas are used, unlike a reduced non-defining relative.

items.prompt: He had already read the novel twice. He found the film adaptation deeply disappointing.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause to give the completed prior reason, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having already read the novel twice, he found the film adaptation deeply disappointing.
Having already read the novel twice, he found the film adaptation deeply disappointing
Having already read the novel twice he found the film adaptation deeply disappointing
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having + past participle' can express a reason rooted in a completed prior event: 'Having already read the novel twice' = 'Because he had already read it twice'. It is the completedness of that earlier reading (marked by 'Having') that explains his later reaction, so a plain -ing clause ('Reading the novel twice') would blur the time relationship.

items.prompt: She was unfamiliar with the local customs. She hesitated to speak first at the meeting.
items.instruction: Combine using an -ing participle clause with 'Being' to express the reason, beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being unfamiliar with the local customs, she hesitated to speak first at the meeting.
Being unfamiliar with the local customs, she hesitated to speak first at the meeting
Being unfamiliar with the local customs she hesitated to speak first at the meeting
items.explanation: A causal clause describing a state ('Because she was unfamiliar with the local customs') reduces to a participle clause with 'Being + complement': 'Being unfamiliar with the local customs'. Here the -ing participle expresses reason rather than a simultaneous action, and the understood subject stays 'she', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: He sat by the fire. He read a novel until midnight.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a present (-ing) participle clause for the simultaneous action, beginning with 'Sitting'.
answer: Sitting by the fire, he read a novel until midnight.
Sitting by the fire he read a novel until midnight
items.explanation: Two simultaneous actions by the same subject are joined by an -ing clause: 'Sitting by the fire' replaces 'While he sat by the fire'. The shared subject 'he' is not repeated, and because that understood subject is also the subject of the main clause the participle does not dangle.

items.prompt: She had already seen the film twice. She declined the invitation to the cinema.
items.instruction: Combine using a perfect participle clause to show the first action was completed first, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having already seen the film twice, she declined the invitation to the cinema.
Having already seen the film twice she declined the invitation to the cinema
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having already seen' makes explicit that the seeing was completed before she declined; here it also carries a causal sense ('because she had already seen it'). A plain -ing form ('Seeing the film twice') would wrongly suggest the two events coincided.

items.prompt: The palace was destroyed during the war. It was never rebuilt.
items.instruction: Combine using an -ed (passive) participle clause, beginning with 'Destroyed'.
answer: Destroyed during the war, the palace was never rebuilt.
Destroyed during the war the palace was never rebuilt
items.explanation: A passive clause ('which was destroyed during the war') reduces to the bare past participle 'Destroyed'. The understood subject is 'the palace', the subject of the main clause, so the fronted modifier attaches correctly and does not dangle.

items.prompt: The documents that were seized during the raid have now been returned to their owners.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (a past/-ed participle) instead of 'that were seized'.
answer: The documents seized during the raid have now been returned to their owners.
The documents seized during the raid have now been returned to their owners
items.explanation: A defining relative clause with 'that/which + be + past participle' reduces to the bare participle: 'that were seized' becomes 'seized'. This is a reduced relative clause modifying 'the documents', not an adverbial one, so no commas are added.

items.prompt: Because he lacked the necessary experience, he was passed over for promotion.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present (-ing) participle clause, beginning with 'Lacking'.
answer: Lacking the necessary experience, he was passed over for promotion.
Lacking the necessary experience he was passed over for promotion
items.explanation: A causal clause ('Because he lacked') reduces to an -ing participle expressing reason: 'Lacking the necessary experience' = 'As he lacked it'. The stative verb 'lack' appears as a participle even though it is not normally used in the progressive, because a reason participle marks cause, not an action in progress.

items.prompt: She had lived in Japan for a decade. She spoke the language fluently.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a perfect participle clause for the completed prior situation, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lived in Japan for a decade, she spoke the language fluently.
Having lived in Japan for a decade she spoke the language fluently
Having lived in Japan for a decade, she spoke the language fluently
items.explanation: The perfect participle 'Having lived' reduces 'She had lived in Japan for a decade' and marks that situation as completed and relevant before the main verb: 'Having lived in Japan for a decade' = 'Because/After she had lived in Japan for a decade'. The understood subject of the participle is 'she', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle. A plain -ing clause ('Living in Japan for a decade') would lose the sense that the residence preceded and explains her fluency.

items.prompt: As soon as she opened the letter, she knew the news was bad.
items.instruction: Combine using an 'On + -ing' participle clause of time, beginning with 'On'.
answer: On opening the letter, she knew the news was bad.
On opening the letter she knew the news was bad
items.explanation: 'On + -ing' is a formal way of saying 'as soon as' or 'immediately when': 'On opening the letter' = 'The moment she opened the letter'. The construction stresses that the main event follows instantly, and its understood subject is 'she', the subject of the main clause.

items.prompt: The medicine will be distributed nationwide as soon as it is approved.
items.instruction: Rewrite the time clause as a reduced clause using 'Once + past participle', beginning with 'Once'.
answer: Once approved, the medicine will be distributed nationwide.
Once approved the medicine will be distributed nationwide
items.explanation: The subordinator 'once' can be kept in front of a bare past participle when the reduced clause is passive: 'once it is approved' becomes 'once approved', dropping the subject and the form of 'be'. The understood subject is 'the medicine', the subject of the main clause, so the clause does not dangle.

items.prompt: She financed her degree. She did so by tutoring younger pupils.
items.instruction: Combine into one sentence using a 'by + -ing' clause of means, beginning 'She financed her degree by…'.
answer: She financed her degree by tutoring younger pupils.
She financed her degree by tutoring younger pupils
items.explanation: A 'by + -ing' clause expresses means — the method by which the main action was accomplished: 'by tutoring younger pupils' answers 'how did she finance it?'. Unlike a simultaneous or result -ing clause, this one is introduced by 'by' and names the instrument or method, with 'she' understood as its subject.

items.prompt: Passengers who hold a valid ticket may board the train at any door.
items.instruction: Rewrite using a reduced relative clause (present participle) in place of 'who hold'.
answer: Passengers holding a valid ticket may board the train at any door.
Passengers holding a valid ticket may board the train at any door
items.explanation: A defining relative clause reduces to an -ing participle even when the verb is habitual or stative rather than progressive: 'who hold a valid ticket' becomes 'holding a valid ticket'. Reduced relatives use the present participle regardless of aspect, so 'holding' here means 'who habitually hold', not 'who are in the act of holding'.

items.prompt: Consider the faulty sentence: 'Walking through the park, the flowers seemed especially beautiful.'
items.instruction: Rewrite so the participle clause no longer dangles, keeping 'Walking through the park,' and giving the main clause the subject 'I' with the verb 'found'.
answer: Walking through the park, I found the flowers especially beautiful.
Walking through the park I found the flowers especially beautiful
items.placeholder: Walking through the park, I found…
items.explanation: The original dangles because the understood subject of 'Walking' would be 'the flowers', yet flowers do not walk. The fix is to make the person who walked the subject of the main clause: with 'I found the flowers…', the implied subject of 'Walking' now matches the main subject 'I', so the modifier attaches correctly.

items.prompt: She was a doctor. She immediately recognised the symptoms.
items.instruction: Combine using a present participle clause of the verb 'be' to express the reason, beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being a doctor, she immediately recognised the symptoms.
Being a doctor she immediately recognised the symptoms
items.explanation: The verb 'be' can itself head a participle clause: 'Being a doctor' reduces 'Because she was a doctor' and gives the reason for the main clause. The understood subject of 'Being' is 'she', which is also the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle. This causal 'Being + noun/adjective' pattern is distinct from a simultaneous action clause; it explains why the main event happens rather than describing something occurring at the same time.

items.prompt: The weather was fine. They decided to set off at dawn.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction (a participle clause carrying its own noun subject, with no 'with'), beginning with 'The weather being'.
answer: The weather being fine, they decided to set off at dawn.
The weather being fine they decided to set off at dawn
items.explanation: A nominative absolute construction has its own explicit subject ('the weather') followed by a participle ('being'), and is grammatically independent of the main clause. Because it carries its own subject, it is not required to share the subject of the main clause ('they') and therefore does not dangle — unlike an ordinary -ing clause, whose understood subject must match that of the main clause. 'The weather being fine' conveys the attendant circumstance 'as/since the weather was fine'.

items.prompt: If it is used sparingly, a single tube of this cream lasts for months.
items.instruction: Rewrite the conditional clause as a past (passive) participle clause of condition, beginning with 'Used'.
answer: Used sparingly, a single tube of this cream lasts for months.
Used sparingly a single tube of this cream lasts for months
items.explanation: A conditional clause ('If it is used sparingly') can reduce to a bare past participle that carries a conditional sense: 'Used sparingly' = 'If/When it is used sparingly'. The understood subject of the participle must be the subject of the main clause, here 'a single tube of this cream', which is indeed what is used, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The work was finally finished. The exhausted team left the office well after midnight.
items.instruction: Combine using an absolute construction with a past participle (its own subject, no 'with'), beginning with 'The work'.
answer: The work finally finished, the exhausted team left the office well after midnight.
The work finally finished the exhausted team left the office well after midnight
items.explanation: The nominative absolute 'The work finally finished' has its own subject ('the work') separate from the subject of the main clause ('the team'), which is exactly why it does not dangle even though the two subjects differ. Unlike a reduced relative clause, an absolute modifies the whole main clause, supplying the circumstance under which the team left, and a past participle is used because the work was finished (passive) rather than doing the finishing.

items.prompt: Driving to work this morning, the traffic was so heavy that I was an hour late.
items.instruction: The participle clause dangles, since the traffic was not driving. Correct it by making the person the subject of the main clause, keeping 'Driving to work this morning'.
answer: Driving to work this morning, I found the traffic so heavy that I was an hour late.
Driving to work this morning I found the traffic so heavy that I was an hour late
items.placeholder: Driving to work this morning, I…
items.explanation: A participle clause takes its subject from the subject of the main clause, so 'Driving to work this morning, the traffic was heavy' wrongly implies the traffic was driving. The repair is to recast the main clause so that its subject ('I') is the one performing the participle's action: 'Driving to work this morning, I found the traffic so heavy…'. The participle itself does not change; only the main clause is rebuilt around the correct subject.

items.prompt: Because he could see that no one was listening to him any longer, he abandoned his speech.
items.instruction: Reduce the reason clause to a present participle clause of reason, beginning with 'Seeing'.
answer: Seeing that no one was listening to him any longer, he abandoned his speech.
Seeing that no one was listening to him any longer he abandoned his speech
items.explanation: A causal clause introduced by 'because' can be reduced to an -ing participle expressing reason, and 'Seeing (that)…' is the idiomatic way to render 'because he could see/realised that…'. The participle retains its own 'that'-complement clause, while its understood subject stays 'he', the subject of the main clause. This is an adverbial participle clause of cause, not a reduced relative.

items.prompt: The old mill could not be saved. Its timbers had been rotted through by decades of damp.
items.instruction: Combine using an absolute construction of the form 'possessive + noun + past participle', beginning with 'Its'.
answer: Its timbers rotted through by decades of damp, the old mill could not be saved.
Its timbers rotted through by decades of damp the old mill could not be saved
items.placeholder: Its timbers rotted through…, the old mill…
items.explanation: This is an absolute construction whose subject is a possessive noun phrase, 'Its timbers', governing the past participle 'rotted through'. Because the absolute carries its own subject, it need not share the subject of the main clause ('the old mill'), so no dangling modifier arises. The past participle is passive in force, showing that the timbers were rotted by the damp rather than doing the rotting themselves.

items.prompt: Because he had lost his passport, he was unable to board the flight.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a perfect participle clause expressing cause, beginning with 'Having'.
answer: Having lost his passport, he was unable to board the flight.
Having lost his passport he was unable to board the flight
items.explanation: A causal clause referring to a completed prior event ('Because he had lost his passport') reduces to a perfect participle clause 'Having + past participle': 'Having lost his passport'. The perfect form makes clear the loss happened before, and is the cause of, the inability to board. The understood subject 'he' is shared with the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The sun had set behind the mountains. The travellers decided to make camp for the night.
items.instruction: Combine using an absolute (nominative absolute) participle construction that keeps its own subject at the front, beginning with 'The sun having set'.
answer: The sun having set behind the mountains, the travellers decided to make camp for the night.
The sun having set behind the mountains the travellers decided to make camp for the night
items.explanation: An absolute construction places a noun ('The sun') immediately before a participle ('having set'), giving the participle clause its own subject that differs from the main clause's subject ('the travellers'). Because the participle carries its own explicit subject, no dangling modifier results even though the subjects differ. The perfect participle 'having set' also marks the setting of the sun as completed before the main action.

items.prompt: As he was new to the company, he was unsure of the correct procedures.
items.instruction: Rewrite the reason clause as a present participle clause using the participle of 'be', beginning with 'Being'.
answer: Being new to the company, he was unsure of the correct procedures.
Being new to the company he was unsure of the correct procedures
items.explanation: A causal clause describing a state ('As he was new to the company') reduces to a present participle clause built on 'being': 'Being new to the company'. Unlike the perfect participle 'Having + past participle', which marks a completed prior event, 'Being + adjective' expresses an ongoing state that is the reason for the main clause. The understood subject is 'he', shared with the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: If you look at it closely, you will notice tiny cracks running through the varnish.
items.instruction: Rewrite the conditional 'if' clause as a present (-ing) participle clause of condition, beginning with 'Looking'.
answer: Looking at it closely, you will notice tiny cracks running through the varnish.
Looking at it closely you will notice tiny cracks running through the varnish
items.explanation: A conditional clause ('If you look at it closely') can reduce to an -ing participle clause when the condition and the main clause share the same subject: 'Looking at it closely' = 'If you look at it closely'. The shared subject 'you' is understood as the subject of the participle, so the clause does not dangle; the conditional (rather than purely simultaneous) reading comes from the context and the modal 'will' in the main clause.

items.prompt: If the weather permits, we will hold the graduation ceremony outdoors.
items.instruction: Rewrite the conditional 'if' clause as an idiomatic absolute participle construction, beginning with 'Weather permitting'.
answer: Weather permitting, we will hold the graduation ceremony outdoors.
Weather permitting we will hold the graduation ceremony outdoors
items.explanation: 'Weather permitting' is a fixed idiomatic absolute construction ('noun + -ing participle') meaning 'if the weather allows it'. Like other absolutes, it carries its own subject ('weather'), distinct from the main clause's 'we', so it does not dangle. The set phrase compresses the whole conditional 'If the weather permits' into two words and is used only in this frozen form.

items.prompt: When it is seen from above, the city resembles a vast circuit board.
items.instruction: Rewrite by fronting a past (passive) participle clause, dropping 'When it is', beginning with 'Seen' and keeping 'the city' as the subject of the main clause.
answer: Seen from above, the city resembles a vast circuit board.
Seen from above the city resembles a vast circuit board
items.explanation: A passive time clause ('When it is seen from above') reduces to the bare past participle 'Seen from above'. The understood subject of 'Seen' must be the subject of the main clause, 'the city' — the thing that is seen — so the fronted modifier attaches correctly and does not dangle.

items.prompt: Once the project has been completed, it will supply clean water to half a million people.
items.instruction: Reduce the time clause to a past participle while keeping the conjunction 'Once', beginning with 'Once completed' and restoring 'the project' as the subject of the main clause.
answer: Once completed, the project will supply clean water to half a million people.
Once completed the project will supply clean water to half a million people
items.explanation: The subordinating conjunction 'Once' can be kept in front of a participle clause for precision: 'Once the project has been completed' reduces to 'Once completed', dropping the subject and the passive 'has been'. The understood subject of 'completed' must head the main clause, so the pronoun 'it' is restored to the noun 'the project' to keep the participle from dangling.

items.prompt: Walking through the door, the smell of fresh bread greeted her.
items.instruction: This sentence dangles: the -ing clause 'Walking through the door' wrongly attaches to 'the smell', which cannot walk. Rewrite it so the person who walks is the subject of the main clause, beginning with 'Walking through the door, she' and using the passive 'was greeted by'.
answer: Walking through the door, she was greeted by the smell of fresh bread.
Walking through the door she was greeted by the smell of fresh bread
items.explanation: The understood subject of a fronted participle must be the subject of the main clause. In the original, that subject is 'the smell of fresh bread', which cannot walk — a classic dangling modifier. Recasting the main clause in the passive ('she was greeted by the smell') puts 'she', the one who actually walks, into subject position, so 'Walking through the door' now attaches to the correct subject.

items.prompt: If the weather permits, the ceremony will be held in the garden.
items.instruction: Rewrite the condition as an absolute participle construction that keeps its own subject, beginning with 'Weather permitting'.
answer: Weather permitting, the ceremony will be held in the garden.
Weather permitting the ceremony will be held in the garden
items.explanation: 'Weather permitting' is an absolute construction meaning 'if the weather permits'. Unlike an ordinary participle clause, an absolute clause retains its OWN subject ('weather'), which differs from the main-clause subject ('the ceremony'). It is precisely this licensed independent subject that stops the participle from dangling.

items.prompt: The new regulations take effect next month, which means all suppliers must be recertified.
items.instruction: Reduce the sentential relative clause 'which means' to a present participle clause, ending with '…, meaning…'.
answer: The new regulations take effect next month, meaning all suppliers must be recertified.
The new regulations take effect next month meaning all suppliers must be recertified
items.explanation: A sentential relative clause ('which means…') that comments on the whole preceding statement reduces to the -ing participle 'meaning…'. Here the participle's understood subject is the entire preceding fact rather than a single noun — a recognised use of the -ing clause to express a consequence, parallel to the result use of 'forcing…'.

items.prompt: The last train had already left. The stranded passengers had no choice but to walk home.
items.instruction: Combine using a nominative absolute construction (a noun with its own perfect participle) for the prior event, beginning with 'The last train having'.
answer: The last train having already left, the stranded passengers had no choice but to walk home.
The last train having already left the stranded passengers had no choice but to walk home
The last train having left, the stranded passengers had no choice but to walk home.
items.explanation: A nominative absolute construction keeps its OWN subject ('the last train') and attaches a participle to it, so unlike an ordinary participle clause it need not share the subject of the main clause. The perfect participle 'having left' marks the departure as completed before the main event, and because the absolute has an explicit subject there is no dangling modifier even though the main subject is 'the passengers'.

items.prompt: Because he was shocked by the sudden news, he could barely speak.
items.instruction: Rewrite reducing the reason clause to a past (passive) participle clause, beginning with 'Shocked'.
answer: Shocked by the sudden news, he could barely speak.
Shocked by the sudden news he could barely speak
items.explanation: A causal passive clause reduces to a bare past participle expressing reason: 'Because he was shocked' becomes 'Shocked'. The form of 'be' and the repeated subject are dropped; the understood subject stays 'he', the subject of the main clause, so the modifier does not dangle.

items.prompt: The soldiers marched into the square. Their rifles were slung across their backs.
items.instruction: Combine using a 'with + noun + past participle' absolute construction for the attendant circumstances, placed after the main clause (', with their rifles…').
answer: The soldiers marched into the square, with their rifles slung across their backs.
The soldiers marched into the square with their rifles slung across their backs
items.explanation: The absolute construction 'With + noun + past participle' supplies attendant circumstances using a passive participle: 'with their rifles slung across their backs' = 'and their rifles were slung across their backs'. Like the -ing absolute, it carries its own subject ('their rifles'), so it need not share the subject of the main clause and does not dangle.
